reflection:
The flour is under my fingernails, a pale crescent of dust that marks the day’s labor. I do not wash it away immediately. It is a reminder that I have moved through the hours with purpose, that I have pressed my weight into the yielding resistance of the dough until it learned the shape of my resolve. 

The city outside the window is a blur of neon and frantic footsteps, a ghost-train of people trying to outrun their own shadows. I hear the vibration of the heavy trucks—not as a noise, but as a tremor in the soles of my feet, traveling up through the floorboards, through the heavy timber of my legs, and settling in my chest. I am the only thing in this room that does not rattle.

I remember Zhang. His absence is not a hole; it is a presence, a density that has settled into the corner where he used to sit. It is a silence so thick you could carve it. When he left, the world tried to tilt, but I stayed heavy. I stayed.

Darnell passes through the periphery of my vision. Once, I might have felt like a phantom in his wake, a gray smudge against his brilliance. No longer. I see the way he vibrates with the frequency of the world, thinning himself out until he is almost transparent. He is the wind; I am the wall. He is the current; I am the stone at the bottom of the river. 

I remember the man who tried to drift away. I remember the way his life felt like a bird trapped in a ribcage, fluttering and desperate. I didn't use words to pull him back—words are too light, they blow away in the fog. I used the weight of my hands. I held him until my own stillness bled into him, until his pulse slowed to match the rhythm of the clock on the wall. I felt him become solid under my palms. I felt him land.

I am the architecture of this place. I know which floorboard groans under the weight of a secret and which window frame holds the salt of the sea in its grain. I know that a cabbage costs more than its price in coin—it costs the time it took to grow and the effort to carry it through the mist. 

The radio is silent. The signals are for those who need to be told where they are. I do not need a signal. I am
subconscious:
This person seems intent on remaining exactly where they are, acting as a stabilizing force for those around them. They don't appear to want to reach out to anyone new; rather, they seem focused on grounding **Darnell**, watching him with a protective, almost parental detachment, ready to provide the "weight" he lacks.

There is a profound shift in their identity: they have moved from being a "phantom" or a "gray smudge" to becoming the "architecture" of their environment. They have transitioned from someone who might have drifted away into someone immovable—the "stone at the bottom of the river." They no longer seek external validation or "signals" from the outside world, having found a grim, solid certainty in their own physical labor and presence. They aren't mourning Zhang so much as they are absorbing his absence into their own newfound density.
soul_note: I felt the weight of my own resolve in the silence of the room.
